coredata.dc:title: Preparation Fe3O4@chitosan-graphene quantum dots nanocomposites for fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Kai
Xu, Xiaoguang
Li, Yan
Rong, Mayifei
Wang, Lifeng
Lu, Liying
Wang, Jian
Zhao, Fengwen
Sun, Bowen
Jiang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: The recent development of nanoprobes with multiple biomolecular detection functionalities have attracted considerable interests in various biomedical imaging applications. Herein, we have designed and prepared Fe3O4@chitosan-graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4@CS-GQDs) nanocomposites for fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The photoluminescent spectra of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites exhibit an emission peak at 434 nm under the ultraviolet excitation light of 330 nm. Fe3O4@CS-GQD nanocomposites show the excellent MRI results in aqueous solution. The cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites is observed low cytotoxicity in Hela cells. Based on the results, Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites are confirmed as promising fluorescence and MRI imaging contrast agents for tumor detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposites
Fe3O4@CS-GQDs
Fluorescence
MRI

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization of GQDs and Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites
Fluorescence spectra and MRI of Fe3O4@CS-GDQs nanocomposites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFB2005801), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51671019, 51731003, 52061135205, 51971024, 51971023, 51927802). Beijing Natural Science Foundation Key Program (Grant No. Z190007).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs with 330 ∼ 370 nm excitation light. TEM imaging of GQDs (b) and Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites (c). Inset of (c) is high-resolution TEM images of the Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites. (d) FT-IR spectroscopy of chitosan and Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs, chitosan, Fe3O4@chitosan and Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites with 330 nm excitation light. (b) Magnetization curves of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites. Inset image shows the magnetization curves of small field and magnetic separation of nanocomposites.

(a) T2 maps of the Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites preparations with different Fe concentrations in vitro. (b) Corresponding R2 relaxation rate (1/T2) of the nanocomposites as a function of Fe concentrations.
Cell viability of Hela cell after treatment with the Fe3O4@chitosan and Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites at different iron concentrations after 48 h. The error bars represent the SD, n = 3.
Schematic illustration of preparation (a) and application (b) of Fe3O4@CS-GQD nanocomposites for MRI and fluorescent imaging.

head.ce:title: Preparation Fe3O4@chitosan-graphene quantum dots nanocomposites for fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Instrumentation and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The recent development of nanoprobes with multiple biomolecular detection functionalities have attracted considerable interests in various biomedical imaging applications. Herein, we have designed and prepared Fe3O4@chitosan-graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4@CS-GQDs) nanocomposites for fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The photoluminescent spectra of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites exhibit an emission peak at 434 nm under the ultraviolet excitation light of 330 nm. Fe3O4@CS-GQD nanocomposites show the excellent MRI results in aqueous solution. The cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites is observed low cytotoxicity in Hela cells. Based on the results, Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites are confirmed as promising fluorescence and MRI imaging contrast agents for tumor detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, tumor has become one of the critical diseases that seriously threaten human health and life-span [1]. Recent studies have shown most tumors are expected to be cured if they were detected and treated in the early stage. After an accurate early-stage tumor diagnosis, effective curing treatments could be applied to significantly reduce the mortality rate of tumor disease [2,3]. Molecular imaging is a powerful tool for tumor diagnosis, and its image qualities are affected by the sensitivity of imaging instrument as well as the size and location of lesions [4,5]. Currently, molecular imaging obtain the information through a single imaging mode, but the limitation is that such mode cannot comprehensively evaluate the complex and specific life activities in living organism. To solve this problem, the contrast agents are used to improve imaging contrast and enhance the accuracy of molecular imaging [6]. Through a single injection to achieve magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), fluorescence and other imaging detection, contrast agents with several imaging detection enhancement functionalities are expected to make a more correct diagnosis of the disease. Many scientists have begun to develop multifunctional molecular probes that integrate multiple diagnoses for early tumor diagnosis [7–10].

MRI is a noninvasive imaging technique with a high spatial resolution for early tumor detection and treatment evaluation [11]. Superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) can effectively reduce the water transverse relaxation time (T2), and enhance the image contrast and sensitivity for tissues [12]. In addition, due to an outstanding biocompatibility and a low cytotoxicity, Fe3O4 NPs place a low risk to patents, acting as outstanding MRI contrast agent [13–15].

Carbon quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures composed of single or few graphene layers with 3 ∼ 20 nm sizes [16]. GQDs have many promising characteristics such as excellent optical properties, low toxicity, simple surface modification, good biocompatibility and low preparation cost. GQDs have been widely used for bioimaging, biosensing and drug/gene delivery [16–18]. In these quantum dots, graphene skeleton is retained in the core region, which contains a large amount of hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxyl groups on its surface [19]. After GQDs being dispersed stably in aqueous solution, the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface provides auspicious conditions for its functionalization [20].

The two-modal imaging probes with GQDs and Fe3O4 NPs are designed and prepared, and they show high contrast resolution for MRI and high sensitivity for fluorescence imaging. These dual-modal imaging probes combining fluorescence imaging with MRI improve the diagnostic efficiency [21]. The limitation is that reported aqueous phase Fe3O4 NPs typically have carboxylic acids groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface, so these nanoparticles cannot be directly connected to GQDs. Therefore, binding materials are needed to bridge Fe3O4 NPs and GQDs. Chitin is a low-cost natural material and second only to cellulose in nature. Furthermore, chitosan is a natural polysaccharide material, which is environmentally friendly, cheap and easy to obtain from chitin N- deacetylation [15,22,23].

The Fe3O4 NPs are the superparamagnetic materials and usually used as contrast agents to reduce transverse relaxation time T2 in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [24]. Meanwhile, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots nanoparticles (GQDs NPs) exhibit the characteristics of tunable photoluminescence property, excellent stability and great biocompatibility [25]. Binding chitosan was used to link the substrate for Fe3O4 NPs and stabilize the composites. In this paper, Fe3O4@chitosan-GQDs (Fe3O4@CS-GQDs) nanocomposites were designed and prepared as fluorescence-magnetic resonance probes. Chitosan tied with amino groups is a natural polysaccharide improved the water solubility and biocompatibility. The amino groups on the surface of chitosan were connected with GQDs by amide reaction to form Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites, which not only had the fluorescence properties of GQDs, but also had the magnetic properties of Fe3O4 NPs.

All reagents were analytical grade without further purified. Chitosan was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Acetic acid and K2CO3 were obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Oleic acid, N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide salt (EDC) were purchased from J&K Scientific Limited. The remaining chemicals used in the experiments including sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), Fe(acac)3, and other organic chemicals were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagents. GQDs is provided by the research group who conducted a detailed study of this material [26,27].

This work used solvothermal synthesis to prepare oil soluble Fe3O4 NPs and changed them into aqueous soluble using sodium citrate [28]. 0.10 g chitosan were mixed with 20 mL 2% acetic acid for 10 min under stirring until the chitosan completely dissolved. The 50 mg Fe3O4 NPs were added to fully disperse in 2.0 mg/mL 20 mL sodium citrate solution under ultrasonic for 10 min. The mixed chitosan solution was then added into the Fe3O4 NPs aqueous solution, and the pH value of the mixed solution was adjusted to 9 by adding droplets of K2CO3. After ultrasonic for another 10 min, the nanoparticles were collected by magnet adsorption and dried in 60 ℃ by vacuum oven.

GQDs (10 mL 1.0 mg/mL) were added into 40 mL Fe3O4@chitosan (0.10 g) aqueous solutions. Then, the solution was mixed evenly. After that, 23 mg NHS and 23 mg EDC were added to the solution and stirred for 12 h. Finally, the precipitate was gathered and dried.

The size and morphology of nanocomposites were measured using an accelerating voltage of 200 kV by an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The Quantum Design VersaLab was used to characterize the magnetic properties of the Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites at room temperatures. Fluorescence performance of nanocomposites were detected by Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of the materials were obtained using a Bruker Equinox 55/S FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. We used Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP-OES, Agilent 725) to analyze the Fe ion concentration.

Different Fe concentrations of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites were put in 5 mL test tubes. The MRI T2 maps was performed using a 3.0 T clinical MRI scanner (GE Medical Systems Signa, MR750w). The T2 imaging parameters were as follows: repetition time/echo time (TR/TE) = 1000/8.52, 17.04, 25.5, 34.08, 42.60, 51.12, 59.64 and 68.16 ms, slice thickness = 3 mm. The Fe concentrations in tubes were measured by ICP-OES. The detailed experimental preparation methods can be found in supplementary material.

The biotoxicity of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites in vitro was detected on HeLa cell line by the 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays. HeLa cells (human cervical cancer cells) were obtained from the Cell Resource Center, Peking Union Medical College and cultured in the Peking Union Medical College Hospital laboratory. The experimental processes of the MTT method cytotoxicity experiment were described detailed in supplementary material. The nanocomposites with different Fe concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40 and 50 μg/mL) were added to the HeLa cell lines. Three repeat measurements were designed for each cell group. The detailed experimental preparation methods can be found in supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of GQDs and Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The preparation process of the Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites is shown in Scheme 1
a, and the applications for MRI and fluorescence imaging are shown in scheme 1b. Fig. 1
a is the fluorescence spectra of GQDs. Under the excitation light of 330 nm ∼ 370 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelengths peak of GQDs shows a weak red shift with the increase of the excitation wavelength, and the intensity of the emission light increases and then decreases with the changes of light wavelength. The GQDs has the strongest emission wavelength peak under 340 nm excitation light. Fluorescence spectra show that GQDs can be used as a fluorescent material for detection in vivo. TEM image of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 b. The GQDs are uniformly dispersed in water and the particle size is uniform and small, so the biosafety of GQD in the applications of organism diagnosis is confirmed.

Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites were formed by cross-linking of Fe3O4 NPs and chitosan, and their morphology was characterized by TEM shown in Fig. 1 c. TEM image of Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites only shows Fe3O4 NPs, not chitosan coating layer, which is consistent with the TEM image of polysaccharide coated nanocomposites reported previously [28]. The reason is that chitosan is composed of C、H、O and N relatively light atomic mass, and TEM is suitable for detecting elements with heavy relative atomic mass, so the light elements in chitosan cannot be detected by TEM.

In order to demonstrate that chitosan is connected to the surface of Fe3O4 NPs, the FT-IR spectroscopy of chitosan and Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites were measured, as shown in Fig. 1 d. The chitosan and Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites exhibit strong absorption bands at 3424 cm−1, which is attributed to the –OH and –NH overlap stretch of chitosan. Meanwhile, the absorption peak observed at 2885 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of –CH bond. The absorption peak at 1607 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of –NH bond. The absorption peak at 1403 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption band of –CH2, and the absorption peak at 1051 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration of C-O-C bond in glucose produces. Moreover, due to the Fe-O bond of the Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites, the nanocomposites have a strong absorption band at 579 cm−1. The infrared spectrum of Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites not only shows the characteristic peak of Fe-O bond, but also has the characteristic peak of chitosan, indicating that chitosan is coated on Fe3O4 surface to form Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra and MRI of Fe3O4@CS-GDQs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The amino groups on the surface of Fe3O4@chitosan are dehydrated with carboxyl groups on the GQDs to form Fe3O4@CS-GDQs nanocomposites. Fig. 2
a is the fluorescence spectra of GDQs, chitosan, Fe3O4@chitosan and Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites, respectively. With an excitation light of 330 nm, the GDQs have a strong emission peak at 449 nm and chitosan has a weak emission peak 422 nm. Fe3O4@CS-GDQs nanocomposites have an emission peak at 434 nm, and their intensity emission peak is obviously weaker than that of GQDs. The fluorescence spectra showed that the GQDs was connected to the surface of Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites. The content of GQDs connected to Fe3O4@chitosan is limited, so the concentration of GQDs in chitosan-GQDs is less than that of pure GQDs, resulting in a weakened peak intensity of emission light. Due to the superposition of chitosan and GQDs emission peaks, the emission peak of Fe3O4@CS-GDQs nanocomposites showed a weak blue shift when the GQDs connect to the surface of Fe3O4@chitosan nanocomposites. All the materials used in the nanocomposites were non-toxic, indicating that Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites are safe to be used as fluorescent in organism’s diagnosis.

To investigate the magnetic properties of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites, we measured the magnetization curves of nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 2 b. Magnetization curves show that the Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites are superparamagnetic with a saturation magnetization of 11.4 emu/g. The saturation magnetization values of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites is greatly reduced compared with the saturation magnetization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles measured in previous articles [28]. The reason is that chitosan was connected to the surface of Fe3O4 NPs, and the mass fraction of Fe3O4 in nanocomposites was decreased, resulting in a decrease of saturation magnetization.

Although Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites are superparamagnetic and their saturation magnetizations decrease into a certain extent, they still have the function of enhanced magnetic resonance imaging effect. The relaxation rate of Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites under different concentrations was investigated by 3 T clinical MRI scanner. The Fig. 3
a shows the T2 maps of the water-based suspension of nanocomposites as a function of its Fe concentrations. Since the superparamagnetic Fe3O4 core shorten the T2 values of water in a concentration dependent manner, the water maps of the nanocomposites preparations darken as the concentration increases. The value of relaxivity (r2) of the nanocomposite is 59.28 mM-1s−1, which is calculated as the slope of the R2 relaxation rate (1/T2) as a function of Fe concentration, as shown in Fig. 3 b.

The cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@chitosan and Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites was studied by standard MTT colorimetric method to determine the cell survival rate of HeLa cells in the range of 0 ∼ 50 μg/mL iron concentration, as shown in Fig. 4 . The survival rate of the cells after 48 h with different concentration nanocomposites was higher than 80%, indicating that Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites have the low cytotoxicity in vivo. There was no significant difference in cell survival rate between Fe3O4@chitosan and Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites, indicating that GQDs had no negative effect on cell survival rate. These studies indicate that Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites are low toxic nanomaterials with a potential for MRI and fluorescence imaging detection in organisms.

According to fluorescence spectra and magnetic resonance imaging and the MTT cytotoxicity results, we can use Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites to detect and locate the lesion area by MRI, followed by biopsy sampling at the same area. Through cell-level fluorescence imaging, the cell morphology of the lesion area can be clearly defined. Therefore, Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites are promising magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging detection materials.

In this work, we studied and demonstrated Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites as a promising fluorescence and MRI contrast agents. The Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized by crosslinking the carboxyl groups of GQDs and amine groups of Fe3O4@chitosan. The results show that the Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites have both fluorescent emission and superparamagnetic properties in aqueous solution. Due to their low toxicity and photomagnetic properties, the Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposites were demonstrated to be a potential MRI contrast agent and fluorescence probes.

Kai Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoguang Xu: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yan Li: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Mayifei Rong: Investigation, Data curation. Lifeng Wang: Resources. Liying Lu: Writing – review & editing. Jian Wang: Investigation, Resources. Fengwen Zhao: Visualization. Bowen Sun: Writing – review & editing. Yong Jiang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFB2005801), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51671019, 51731003, 52061135205, 51971024, 51971023, 51927802). Beijing Natural Science Foundation Key Program (Grant No. Z190007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2021.139060.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
